title: Coping with Anxiety from an Islamic Perspective
text: Anxiety is one of the most common struggles Muslims face today, and it is important to recognize that experiencing it does not mean a weakness in faith. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) himself taught supplications for moments of distress and worry, showing that these feelings are a natural part of the human condition. When the heart feels tight, the Quran reminds us that true tranquility comes through the remembrance of Allah. Simple practices can help: regular dhikr, especially in the morning and evening, grounds the heart in something larger than the immediate worry. Salah, performed with presence, serves as a daily anchor that pulls the mind out of racing thoughts. Reading even a few verses of the Quran slowly, reflecting on their meaning, can soften an anxious heart. Alongside these spiritual practices, Islam does not discourage seeking help from qualified mental health professionals. Seeking treatment for anxiety is no different than seeking treatment for any physical illness — the Prophet said, for every disease Allah has created a cure. If your anxiety is persistent, overwhelming, or interfering with daily life, please speak with a doctor or therapist. Faith and professional care are not in competition; they support each other.

title: Grief and the Promise of Reunion
text: Losing someone you love is one of the deepest pains a human being can experience. Islam does not ask us to suppress grief or pretend we are unaffected. When the Prophet Muhammad's (peace be upon him) own son Ibrahim passed away, his eyes filled with tears, and he said that the heart grieves and the eyes shed tears, but we say only what pleases our Lord. This shows that tears and sorrow are not failures of faith — they are expressions of love. What Islam offers the grieving heart is a framework of meaning. Death is not the end; it is a transition. The promise of reunion in the hereafter is real, and every moment of patience through grief is recorded with Allah. The phrase 'Inna lillahi wa inna ilayhi raji'un' — to Allah we belong and to Him we return — is not just words to say at a funeral. It is a complete worldview. It reminds us that the ones we love were never truly ours to keep; they were entrusted to us for a time, and returning them to their Creator is part of the arrangement we all agreed to. Grief has no timetable. Some days will be heavier than others, even years later. Be patient with yourself, keep the person alive through du'a on their behalf, and trust that your sabr is seen.

title: When Faith Feels Distant
theme: spiritual dryness, iman, connection with Allah, doubt
text: Many practicing Muslims go through periods where worship feels mechanical, du'a feels unheard, and the closeness to Allah they once felt seems to have disappeared. This experience is so common that classical scholars gave it a name and treated it as a normal stage of the spiritual journey, not a sign of failure. Iman naturally rises and falls. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) told his companions that iman wears out in the heart the way clothing wears out, and we should ask Allah to renew it. The first step during a dry season is to keep showing up. Continue praying even when the prayer feels empty. Continue reading Quran even when the verses do not seem to move you. The act itself is worship, even without the accompanying feeling. Second, examine what in your life may be crowding out your spiritual attention — excessive screen time, unresolved sin, sleep deprivation, toxic relationships. The heart is not a muscle that works in isolation; it responds to what you feed it. Third, reach out. Speak with a trusted imam, a knowledgeable friend, or a spiritually-oriented therapist. Isolation deepens spiritual dryness. Finally, know that the very fact that you are distressed by feeling distant from Allah is itself a sign that your iman is alive. A truly dead heart would not notice the distance.

title: Identity and Belonging as a Young Muslim
text: Young Muslims, especially those living as minorities or navigating multiple cultural contexts, often feel caught between worlds. There can be pressure from family to uphold one version of Muslim identity, pressure from peers to assimilate into another, and internal questions about who you really are beneath all of it. These feelings are valid and widely shared. The first thing to understand is that your identity as a Muslim is not the same as any single culture's expression of Islam. The ummah includes Malays, Arabs, Bangladeshis, West Africans, Americans, Bosnians, and converts from every background. The core — belief in Allah, the Prophet, the Quran, the pillars — is shared. The cultural clothing around that core varies, and that is not a problem. It is possible to be authentically Muslim without replicating the exact cultural expression of your parents or your peers. Second, community matters enormously. If the Muslim spaces available to you feel alienating, keep searching. Look for study circles, youth groups, or online communities where you can think out loud without judgment. Finding even one or two friends who share both your faith and your context can transform how grounded you feel. Third, give yourself time. Identity formation is a years-long process, not a weekend decision. The questions you are asking now are the same questions thoughtful Muslims have asked in every generation.

title: Stress, Tawakkul, and Letting Go of Control
theme: stress, trust in Allah, tawakkul, control, worry about the future
text: Modern life rewards the illusion of control. We plan, optimize, track, and worry — and when outcomes do not match our plans, the stress can become consuming. Islam offers a different relationship with control through the concept of tawakkul: genuine reliance on Allah. Tawakkul is often misunderstood as passivity, as if a person who trusts Allah simply does nothing and waits. This is incorrect. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) was asked by a man whether he should leave his camel untied and rely on Allah, and the Prophet replied: tie your camel, then rely on Allah. Tawakkul is the state of the heart after you have done your part. It is the release that follows the effort, not a substitute for it. Practically, this means doing your work, making your plans, and preparing for what you can prepare for — and then handing the outcome to Allah with the recognition that you were never truly in charge of the outcome anyway. The relief this brings is not theoretical. When you truly internalize that results belong to Allah, the grip of anxiety loosens. You still care about your work, your family, your health, your future. But you stop carrying a weight that was never yours to carry. If stress is currently overwhelming you, try this exercise: name one thing you are anxious about, identify the single next action within your control, do that action, and then — consciously, out loud if needed — hand the rest to Allah. Repeat as often as needed. This is tawakkul in practice.
